# Transcrição: 04-Calibration Testing

- **Fonte:** 04-Calibration Testing
- **Duração:** 15:17
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:02]

So in this video, I'm going to show you the concept of account calibration. So the purpose of account calibration is to find out how we can target our users in the most cost-effective way, whilst we're in the testing phase. So if you've been through the graduation testing framework already, you'll know that there are three core parts of the process. Phase one is audience testing, phase two is creative testing, phase three is prospecting and scaling. So for phases one and two, regardless of what my goal is for the business, I want to find a pixel event that's going to allow me to spend the least amount of money and get the biggest outcome. Now, this goes back many, many years when I used to work with many small startups. I'm talking, you know, 2013, 2014, 2015, that kind of period when I didn't really have much budget to spend. And sometimes, you know, I'd work with small companies that wouldn't want to risk more than $10 a day. Or, you know, they might have a 5K monthly budget, but they are still expecting profit in 30 days and things like that. So this strategy, you came out of that. Now, if you're in a position where you're not really counting the pennies, then this may not apply to you. So this is not a strategy you absolutely have to use. If you've got a bit of leeway, some kind of space to be able to spend without worrying about, you know, am I making a return from this? So for example, you know, we've worked with clients that will say, right, here's $10,000 just for testing. And they fully understand that testing means, hey, we might not generate any sell. Now, obviously with 10K spend, you'd expect something back. But, you know, even when I'm spending my own cash on my own e-commerce ventures or info products or anything like that, for me, test is test. I don't expect, even if I put $10,000 into testing, if nothing came back in terms of revenue, but we made a ton of learnings and we were able to go through some hypotheses to prove them or disprove them. That's what testing is about. It's about investing in learning something that's going to help us later on. So let's take it back. So by this point, you're either running ads for yourself or for a company you're working for or for clients you're working with. And this is about being able to find a way to kind of balance your budget effectively. So if I show you what's inside account calibration, you can then decide if this is the right thing for you and whether you need it. So first of all, I'm going to show you a slight change in our approach as of 2021, thanks to iOS changes and Apple, that what I do when I do my account calibration is I split it between iOS devices and Android, first of all, and there'll be a separate video on how that is set up and why we do that. But specifically for this video, I'll just focus on the Android one.

## [03:02 - 06:07]

So what I do is I select one audience, ideally one audience that I know is fairly strong. And then I duplicate that one audience based on how many pixel events there are in my funnel, and therefore how many ad sets I need to test. So for this one, for example, I know that this look like 1.1 to 3% of purchases in the US that 55 plus male or female are my one of my top audiences. So what I'm going to do here is test the event which is purchase at a cart, add payment info, initiate checkout on view content using the seven day click. Now you can use one day click, you can use seven day click for this is really about what the average time is for someone to purchase and convert. In this particular case, we have average order values of $80, give or take. Seven days generally work better for this account. I would encourage most of you to test that. So if you have data and you know how one day click works and seven day click, you can choose the one that you know that works. For me, I test them. So this account, I know that seven day works. If I don't, then I would test seven day versus one day in a Facebook split test using the same audience and see which one gives me the best performance. So just coming back to account calibration, just to recap, we have five audiences for our specific purposes, which have five different events. So apart from that, they're all exactly the same. They're targeting the same audiences, same excludes, only focused on the Facebook newsfeed because I don't want to then have traffic from different sources. I just want to see, like for like, how the performance looks. And then within each of these is one ad and one ad only. And they are exactly the same ad in every single ad set. So I'm minimizing the noise and the only thing that's different for all of these ad sets and the ads is the event. So as you'll know by now, if you've been through the earlier parts of the training, the event defines what kind of person is on the other side. So view content, people that are likely to click, maybe not in buyer mode. Purchases are people that Facebook and Meta have identified as being active purchases. So maybe the purchase in the last 30 days through an ad or through a website. So there's different levels of types of users I'm bringing in. So why do we run the account calibration test? Here's the thing. So if you look at performance and clicks and look at our CPMs, this is typical of what you might see for an e-commerce website. And again, you can run this off any funnel. So your funnel might be view count content because you've got a lander. Then it might be a checkout because you're selling a low ticket item and then a purchase. So your funnel might be a view content, initiate checkout purchase. For someone else, you might be just running leads, in which case it could be someone lands on the landing page with view content and then converts in terms of sign up and then fires the registration complete or lead pixel.

## [06:07 - 09:08]

So what your funnel looks like will be specific for you. But this is typical of what I see. Now, the more valuable the event is, the more you're going to pay. This is just common across Facebook, Instagram, and many other ad platforms as well. So, you know, for example, if we want to go after people that are likely to fire the view content pixel, which is people that generally hit the landing page, then it's not going to cost so much because competition is lower and also the audience size is bigger. Whereas at the top, those that are likely to purchase, competition is higher and the audience size is smaller. That's what defines your cost, your cost per thousand impressions. So if I'm bidding, let's say, let's take it back now. Previously, I said to you, you don't have to use this process if you have essentially money to burn. So if you're, you know, you're running your own budget or the client has said to you, just go and spend this and create the best outcomes so that we can start profiting afterwards. Then you might, you know, if you're running an e-commerce store, just optimize for purchase because that's the only pixel event you care about because that's the event that's going to generate revenue and things like that. Then you might go ahead and just optimize for purchase and go straight into your Phase 1 and Phase 2 testing. But you're going to probably pay twice as much. You know, this is just for this particular account, but it's quite common because purchase costs twice as much as generally the other pixel events. And that's due to the competition is auction dynamics and things like that. My goal is how can I find the cheapest event that also gives me good data as to which audience and which ad is working together? That's all I want to know at this stage. I don't want to know which is the highest performing terms of return on ad spend, cost per acquisition and things like that. Because the ad is there to get good quality, high quality clicks into your funnel, but there's also funnel optimization that needs to go on as well. So if you look at each of these here, the $53 expensive purchase pixel event has given me a 3% click through rate. Now that 3% doesn't mean that the ad's bad. It just means that for people that are in purchase mode, 3% that saw the ad have decided to click. Now, by the way, 3% is also good click through rate, but I'm actually getting more clicks for my money on other purchase events. So I'm considering this as well. I'm not saying that, you know, a high click through rate means that you've got a great ad and a great audience. But these are things I'm using alongside the other data. And here, like if we look at our cost per click, for example, this is what it comes down to. When you have a test budget, are you prepared to pay $1.70 for example for every click you get on that website? Or do you want to pay, you know, a third of a dollar, 33 cents for every click? Because for one click there, I can generate, what is that, five clicks for the same amount of money. Those five clicks will potentially give me more data.

## [09:08 - 12:10]

But obviously I don't want low quality clicks coming through as well. And that's why I also track what success rate I'm getting through that funnel as well. So as an example here, let me just expand that out. We have, so we actually generated a purchase on the ad to cart pixel event and also initiate checkout. Now, these were only one purchases for each of those events. Now, I only run my calibration test for a number, a set number of impressions. So impressions is how many times the ad was served to an individual or how many times the ad was served in the news feed or wherever you're advertising in total. So if we look here, I've got 5,000 impressions for purchase, 5,000 for add to cart, etc., etc. So generally I aim in the calibration part of the testing to get 5,000 impressions at least to get me good enough data. The reason for that is that there comes a point where you have enough data and you don't need to spend more, but there's also the flip side where you don't have enough data to make sense of. And from my experience, that's about 5,000 impressions. So once I've got that, then I can start to make some assumptions about the data coming through. So from that 5,000 impressions, the two of the ad sets have generated a sale. They are not statistically relevant because one sale is not enough to say, hey, this is an amazing pixel event we need to work on. I can also use that as part of my decision making as well. Now, if we go back and look at some of the numbers through the funnel, then we can look here and see what our cost per content view is to get someone to the product page, what the cost to get someone to the cart page is, what the cost to get someone to the initiate checkout is, etc. And really for 5,000 impressions for this kind of funnel, I would be looking at cost per initiate checkout. So for this, for example, I'm looking for a clue that tells me there's a good quality pixel event that I should be testing against. It might be that when I come to running my scaling and my prospecting that I changed the event back to purchase. But FYI, for this account, we actually ran initiate checkout through calibration, which gave us the best performance all around. It was one of the cheapest pixel events to run. So the CPM was about $20, $25. The cost per click was about $0.33. We're getting a high click through rate. And actually, if you look at these numbers, they're really, really strong. So $5 to get someone to add to cart, $13 to get them to checkout, $36 to get the payment info. And this was just going to a single product without a backend funnel built. But when I now plug that into my main campaigns and I'm getting $80 average order values,

## [12:10 - 15:11]

then all of a sudden this campaign is going to be quite profitable. So my conclusion from this is that the initiate checkout gives me the balance between low cost in the auction and high response rate on the ads. But it also is further down my funnel to give me more confidence that if I run testing here, I can find ads and audiences that will give me a good cost per initiate checkout. This is high intent. These are people that have gone through my landing page, they've added to cart and they've considered purchasing as well. Now, some of you might say, yeah, but you know, there's not enough purchases and I want to run more testing and things like that. The reason I don't go too far into that is, guess what? I'm also running retargeting ads. So some of these sales of people that are clicking this far will come through retargeting. So naturally, if I can bring in and let's say, for example, if I run this into prospecting and scaling and I'm still getting people to my checkout for $13, then my retargeting ads will only make my performance overall at the account level better. So the key takeaways are to test your different events, to make sure that you run enough traffic to get at least 5,000 impressions. It doesn't matter if you run $5 a day or $50 or $100 a day, just give it at least 24 hours to collect those 5,000 impressions. That's the main thing I'm looking for. So if you spend whatever amount it is and you get 5,000 impressions in one hour, then that's not really enough data. You want 24-hour data spread there. Run that test. Exactly the same ad, same audiences, just the event changed. Look at your auction performance. So that's your CPM, that's your CTR, that's your CPC. Just to compare what you're seeing. In most cases for e-commerce, you'll see these kind of splits in terms of CPM and costs. And then have a look at your funnel metrics to see what kind of performance you're getting through the funnel. So then you can make a decision like this on which pixel event to go for. And for this particular campaign and this account, we decided to go for initiate checkout for our testing. So phase one testing, when we're trying to find good audiences, and phase two testing, when we're trying to find good ads, optimize for initiate checkout. And for this campaign, for this account, when we then went into phase three, which is now we've got great audiences and great ads, we also tested initiate checkout versus purchase. And initiate checkout still performed better. And part of that is because purchase was so expensive for this audience. And the audience size is smaller, so there's more competition and more cost. But initiate checkout is not that far off purchase. And also because our retargeting was really strong, we were able to bring more sales in. So that's really how account calibration works. And that's how we set it up.

## [15:11 - 15:17]

And it's just a recap worth doing if you want to maximize your test budget.
